metadata.summary: Generate business ideas using 7 frameworks, score on market size/fit/competition/monetization/MVP speed.

content.title: Idea Generation
content.summary: A methodology for systematically generating viable business ideas using 7 frameworks: Skills Inventory, Pain Point Mining, Job Substitution, Productized Service, Unbundling, Market Stacking, and Your Own Problems. The rule: run all 7 frameworks, generate at least 20 candidates, then score each on Market Size, Personal Fit, Competition, Monetization, and Speed to MVP before selecting anything to validate.

why.p: Waiting for inspiration produces nothing. Running only one framework locks the founder into a single idea class (e.g., only Reddit pain points). The scoring matrix prevents personal excitement from overriding weak fundamentals, and generating 20+ candidates before scoring provides enough comparison points for the matrix to produce signal.

list.item: Solopreneur in blank-page mode: skills inventory exists, no idea selected yet.
Quarterly idea-refresh sprint: generate 20+ candidates, score, pick 1-2 to validate.
Combining a known skill with current pain-point data to surface SaaS angles.
Repurposing a portfolio of past services into productized offerings (Framework 4).
You already have a validated idea and paying users — switch to roadmap and feature discovery.
Market sizing or financial projections are needed — this methodology is generation, not evaluation.
Highly technical R&D (deep tech, hardware) where pain-mining and Upwork gig analysis don't apply.
Burnout or decision-fatigue state — adding more options makes the situation worse.

section.name: seven-idea-generation-frameworks
section.p: Seven systematic frameworks for generating business ideas, with templates and worked examples for each.

rule.statement: Start by mapping technical, domain, and soft skills; then ask what problems each skill cluster can solve — not what products you could build.
rule.rationale: Problem-first thinking from existing skills avoids the "solution looking for a problem" failure mode. You already have the domain credibility to execute.

example.title: Skills Inventory framework
example.code: Skill: Python + Marketing analytics Problem: Small businesses have no affordable analytics automation Idea: Automated social media analytics tool for small businesses

section.p: Search Reddit (sort by "top" in niche subreddits), Twitter/X rants, App Store 1-2 star reviews, Quora questions, and forum complaints. For each pain point, document: Pain / Frequency / Current Solutions / Gap / Idea.

rule.statement: List tedious tasks people pay others to do (Upwork gigs, Fiverr, outsourced small-business work) and apply the formula: "Automate [repetitive task] for [specific audience]".
rule.rationale: People who hire for repetitive work have already proven willingness to pay. Automating what they outsource produces a validated demand signal.

section.p: Turn services into products: identify the repeatable part of a service you or others provide, then package it. Consulting → Course. Custom dev → SaaS template. Design work → Design system.

rule.statement: Identify one specific use case of a large platform (Excel, Notion, Salesforce) that deserves a focused tool; build only that.
rule.rationale: Large platforms are designed for breadth, not depth. Niche users are underserved and willing to pay for a tool that actually fits their workflow.

section.p: Combine two niches: Niche A x Niche B = New opportunity. Examples: AI x Legal = Contract analysis. Fitness x Remote work = Home workout for developers. Crypto x Freelancing = Payment platform.

antipattern.description: Defaulting to "AI x [anything]" without genuine domain fit or a specific underserved user problem.
antipattern.reason: LLMs over-produce this pattern. "AI-powered X" is not a business idea; it is a feature. The underlying job must be validated independently.

rule.statement: Document problems you face daily; if you have the problem and can't find a solution, assume others do too.
rule.rationale: Your own problems give you authentic domain knowledge, built-in motivation, and access to a test audience (your network).

rule.statement: Score every idea on 5 criteria (Market Size 20%, Personal Fit 25%, Competition 15%, Monetization 20%, Speed to MVP 20%) before selecting any idea to validate.
rule.rationale: Scoring before selecting prevents personal excitement from dominating. 20+ ideas are needed for the matrix to produce signal — below that, there are too few comparison points.

list.item: Market Size (20%): 5 = billion-dollar opportunity, 1 = tiny niche.
Personal Fit (25%): 5 = excited to work on this daily, 1 = dreading the work.
Competition (15%): 5 = blue ocean, 1 = dominated by well-funded giants.
Monetization (20%): 5 = clear revenue path, 1 = unclear how to charge.
Speed to MVP (20%): 5 = 2 weeks, 1 = 1+ year to validate.

rule.statement: Score Personal Fit before viewing any market data; excitement in market context contaminates the honest answer.
rule.rationale: Personal Fit has the highest weight (25%) and is the only criterion an LLM cannot score. If the agent fills it in, the row must be discarded and re-scored by the founder.

antipattern.description: Waiting for the perfect idea before starting research.
antipattern.reason: Inspiration does not precede research; research produces inspiration. Generate 10+ ideas, score them, pick the best one to explore further.

antipattern.description: Running only one or two of the seven frameworks.
antipattern.reason: Different frameworks surface different idea classes. Skipping frameworks locks the output into a single idea type.

antipattern.description: Not documenting every idea, even obviously bad ones.
antipattern.reason: The rejected list compounds over months. Patterns in rejected ideas often reveal the best path forward 6-12 months later.

antipattern.description: Quoting global TAM as Market Size instead of ICP segment size.
antipattern.reason: A $100B global market is irrelevant for a solopreneur product. Constrain Market Size to the actual ICP segment the product can realistically reach.

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: The 5-criteria scorecard (Market, Fit, Competition, Monetization, MVP-speed) is an unvalidated rubric; weights drift between agents and humans. "Personal Fit = excited daily" is unmeasurable without time. LLMs cannot evaluate it; only the founder can. Market Stacking (#6) tends to produce gimmicks ("AI × X") when run by an LLM — pattern-matching beats substance. "Speed to MVP" is dramatically miscalibrated for AI-augmented dev; the README's 5 = 2 weeks anchor is now closer to days for many ideas. Agents over-index on Pain Point Mining (#2) and ignore Skills Inventory (#1) and Your Own Problems (#7) — the most personal frameworks have the highest hit rate but require non-LLM input.

list.item: LLMs gravitate to "AI agent for X" / "GPT-powered Y" — explicit gimmick_risk flag and a filter agent are mandatory.
Agents inflate Market Size scores by quoting global TAM. Constrain to the actual ICP segment.
Personal Fit cannot be scored by an LLM. If the agent fills it in anyway, throw the row out and have the founder re-score.
Agents recycle the same SaaS tropes (CRM-for-X, Notion-for-Y) — diversify by framework explicitly: prompt one agent at a time per framework.
Date drift: trend-based ideas decay fast. Always include a "valid as of" date in the persisted record.
Human checkpoint: before any selected idea moves to validation, founder reviews the dedupe step — agents collapse genuinely-different ideas into one.
Don't let one agent both generate AND score — generators self-rate too high. Use separate agents (or sessions) for the two phases.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Generation is human-LLM hybrid. Founder fills Skills Inventory and Own Problems offline (LLM cannot). Agent then runs the other five frameworks in parallel — one agent per framework — and produces 5-10 ideas each. A converger agent dedupes, removes obvious gimmicks, and outputs ≤20 candidates. The founder scores Personal Fit; agent scores the other four columns from market data. Final pick is collaborative.

list.item: faion-idea-generator-agent — canonical agent named in the README; orchestrates the 7 frameworks.
faion-pain-point-researcher-agent — feeds Framework #2 with pain points + scores from pain-point-research/.
faion-brainstorm (skill) — diverge/converge over the unioned idea list; native multi-agent.
A custom unbundling-finder (sonnet) — scans a target platform's feature list and proposes unbundled niches.
A custom gimmick-filter (sonnet) — flags "X but with AI" / "Uber for X" patterns.

section.p: Prompt pattern: Read skills/faion/knowledge/solo/research/researcher/idea-generation/README.md. Run frameworks 2 (Pain Mining), 3 (Job Substitution), 4 (Productized), 5 (Unbundling), 6 (Stacking) for audience=<X>, founder skills=<list>. Output a JSON list of ideas with {name, framework, problem, audience, rough_solution, gimmick_risk:0-1}.
Score these ideas on Market/Competition/Monetization/Speed (5-pt). Leave PersonalFit column blank — founder will fill. Compute weighted total assuming PersonalFit=3 placeholder. Sort desc.

list.item: Run all 7 frameworks even if one feels productive. Different frameworks surface different idea classes; skipping bias-locks the founder.
Generate at least 20 ideas before scoring. Below that the matrix has no signal — too few comparison points.
Score Personal Fit first, before any market data. Excitement in market context contaminates the founder's honest answer.
Time-box generation (≤4 hours per session). Beyond that, agents repeat themselves and ideas converge.
Persist every idea, even bad ones. The "Weekly Idea Capture" template compounds over months — patterns emerge from the rejected list.
Pair with pain-point-research first — pain-data fuels Framework #2 with concrete inputs instead of LLM speculation.
Re-evaluate scores quarterly. "Speed to MVP" especially shifts with AI tooling; an idea that scored 2 last year may be 5 today.

template.purpose: Session template: frameworks used, per-idea fields, top-3 to validate, next steps.
Weekly log: problems encountered, online complaints seen, Upwork gigs noted.
